description: Based on the provided text extraction summary, this figure displays scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs of Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels tempered at 600°C and 800°C, illustrating diverse austenite decomposition mechanisms across alloy compositions. For 9Al-10Mn-1C at 600°C, nearly complete discontinuous precipitation produces fine γ+κ lamellae with narrow inter-lamellar spacing, correlating with high hardness (439 HV). At 800°C, the same alloy exhibits cellular transformation generating coarse α+κ lamellae alongside spinodal decomposition featuring nano-sized acicular κ-carbides in residual γ grains. High-Al alloys (12Al-10Mn-1C) show globular κ-carbides in carbon-depleted γ bands at 600°C and wide cellular lamellae at 800°C (399 HV). High-Mn alloys (20-30% Mn) display either no austenite transformation with only grain boundary precipitates at 600°C, or dominant spinodal decomposition with dense cuboidal nano-κ carbides at 800°C. The images reveal δ-ferrite boundaries, precipitate morphologies, and lamellar colonies, though the original image provided is a 'Check for updates' icon with unreadable scientific content.

This figure presents optical micrographs illustrating discontinuous precipitation in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels after tempering at 600°C for 30 minutes. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure of alloy 9Al-10Mn-0.4C, where partial discontinuous precipitation occurs: γ austenite grains undergo transformation into γ+κ lamellar colonies nucleating at grain boundaries, while distinct band-shaped untransformed γ regions remain visible within the microstructure. Subfigure (b) depicts alloy 9Al-10Mn-1C exhibiting nearly complete discontinuous precipitation, where the majority of austenite volume has transformed into fine γ+κ lamellar structures, potentially containing some α-phase, with only minimal residual band-shaped γ regions surviving. The contrast demonstrates that higher carbon content (1 wt% vs 0.4 wt%) significantly enhances the extent of discontinuous precipitation. These microstructural differences correlate with hardness variations reported in the study, establishing that chemical composition provides the critical thermodynamic driving force for κ-carbide precipitation and controls austenite stability during tempering.

This figure presents two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) secondary electron images (20 kV, ×10,000 magnification, 1 μm scale bar) illustrating discontinuous precipitation of γ + κ-carbide lamellae in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels tempered at 600°C. Subfigure (a) shows the 9Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy, where discontinuous precipitation initiates from grain boundaries and propagates into the austenite matrix as fine lamellar colonies, leaving band-shaped untransformed γ regions. Subfigure (b) depicts the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy, revealing nearly complete transformation of austenite into fine γ + κ lamellae with short inter-lamellar spacing, forming fan-shaped cellular colonies that dominate the microstructure. The comparison demonstrates that increasing carbon content from 0.4 wt% to 1 wt% significantly enhances the extent of discontinuous precipitation, producing finer, more densely packed carbide lamellae. According to the citation context, the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy exhibits substantial hardening (439 HV) associated with these fine lamellae, whereas the lower-carbon variant shows only partial transformation. Additionally, the non-discontinuously precipitated austenite in 9Al-10Mn-1C may contain nano-sized κ-carbide precipitation as suggested by EDS analysis. These micrographs support the conclusion that carbon content critically controls the kinetics, morphology, and mechanical property evolution during discontinuous precipitation in these lightweight steels.

Figure 3 comprises two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) secondary electron images (20 kV, ×10,000 magnification, 1 μm scale bar) illustrating the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels subjected to heat treatment at 600°C. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the 12Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy (caption reports 2Al-10Mn-0.4C, likely a typographical error for 12Al consistent with the paper context) and reveals a multifaceted microstructure characterized by globular κ-carbide precipitates embedded within carbon-depleted austenite regions, with precipitation concentrated along δ/γ boundaries and within ferrite. Subfigure (b) presents the 12Al-10Mn-1C alloy, displaying well-developed γ+κ-carbide lamellar structures indicative of discontinuous precipitation, featuring alternating lamellae of austenite and κ-carbides. The contrast between the globular precipitation morphology in (a) and the cellular lamellar structure in (b) demonstrates that higher carbon content (1C versus 0.4C) at 12% Al drives a transition from multifaceted precipitation to discontinuous lamellar transformation, which correlates with enhanced hardness (420 HV for 12Al-10Mn-1C) and distinct austenite decomposition kinetics.

This scanning electron micrograph displays the microstructure of 12Al-10Mn-1C Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel tempered at 600°C for 30 minutes, illustrating the precipitation transformation phenomenon. The image reveals distinct microstructural bands labeled as γ+κ lamellae alongside regions marked γ+coarse κ (kappa carbide), with a scale bar of 100 μm. According to the caption and citation context, this alloy exhibits a complex random-like transformation rather than ordered discontinuous precipitation, featuring alternating bands of two different austenite grades—specifically carbon-depleted austenite containing globular κ-carbides embedded in the matrix. The microstructure lacks the classical fine lamellar structure typical of discontinuous precipitation. This transformation was linked to slight hardening effects, with the alloy achieving 420 HV Vickers hardness. The high aluminum content (12 wt%) increases thermodynamic gradients during tempering, promoting austenite decomposition reactions involving the entire γ volume.

The figure presents a comparative microstructural analysis of four Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel alloys—(a) 9Al-10Mn-1C, (b) 12Al-10Mn-1C, (c) 12Al-20Mn-1C, and (d) 12Al-30Mn-1C—after tempering at 800°C for 30 minutes, illustrating the compositional dependence of austenite decomposition mechanisms. The upper panels show optical microscope micrographies (scale bars 100–200 μm) revealing grain-scale lamellar structures and phase distributions, while the lower panels display secondary electron SEM images (2500×–5000× magnification, 10 μm scale bars) capturing nanoscale precipitate morphologies. Specifically, 9Al-10Mn-1C exhibits a mixed microstructure of cellular transformation products (widespread κ-carbide + α-ferrite + residual-austenite lamellae) coexisting with spinodal-decomposed regions; 12Al-10Mn-1C shows complete cellular transformation into coarse α+κ lamellae with wide inter-lamellar spacing and scattered δ-ferrite bands; 12Al-20Mn-1C presents a hybrid structure where some austenite grains undergo cellular transformation forming wide, thick lath-like κ-carbide lamellae while others exhibit spinodal decomposition; and 12Al-30Mn-1C demonstrates fully spinodal-decomposed austenite containing fine, acicular nano-sized κ-carbide agglomerates. The sequence demonstrates that increasing Mn content from 10% to 30% progressively suppresses cellular transformation in favor of spinodal decomposition, while elevated Al content (12% vs. 9%) promotes coarser lamellar structures with larger inter-lamellar spacing, directly influencing hardness values reported in the study.

Figure 6 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the 9Al-10Mn-1C lightweight steel after tempering at 800°C for 30 minutes, revealing two distinct austenite decomposition mechanisms. Subfigures (a) and (b) at magnifications of ×1,000 and ×5,000 respectively demonstrate spinodal decomposition within untransformed austenite grains, showing nano-sized κ-carbide (E2₁ structure) precipitates appearing as acicular agglomerates dispersed in the γ matrix. Subfigures (c) and (d) at ×5,000 and ×10,000 magnifications illustrate cellular transformation, displaying a lamellar structure composed of κ-carbides, α-ferrite, and residual austenite, where κ-carbides manifest as wide, thick lath-like strips with broad inter-lamellar spacing. The contrast between the fine nano-scale spinodal precipitates and the coarse cellular lamellae explains the moderate hardness value of 356 HV reported for this condition, distinguishing it from the finer discontinuous precipitation microstructures observed at 600°C. This figure supports the conclusion that elevated tempering temperatures (800°C) promote cellular transformation alongside spinodal decomposition in high-carbon Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys.

This scanning electron micrograph displays the microstructure of the 12Al-20Mn-1C lightweight steel alloy after tempering at 800°C, revealing wide and thick lath-like κ-carbide lamellae formed through complete austenitic decomposition. The image, captured at 5,000× magnification with a 5 μm scale bar and 20 kV accelerating voltage, shows distinct lamellar colonies with varying orientations—some appearing as parallel strip-like structures while others exhibit curved morphologies. According to the caption and citation context, these lamellae represent the cellular transformation product consisting of κ-carbide + ferrite (α) + residual austenite (γ), coexisting with spinodal decomposition features in other regions. The microstructure is characterized by broader and thicker κ-carbide strips compared to the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy, resulting in wide inter-lamellar spacing. The left portion of the image shows a region with fine grid-like patterns possibly representing spinodally decomposed areas or surface topography variations. This microstructural morphology supports the finding that despite the increased inter-lamellar spacing at 12% aluminum content, the alloy maintains consistent hardening effects (404 HV) due to the substantial volume fraction of κ-carbide precipitates, demonstrating the balance between Mn-γ stabilization and Al-C driving force for κ-carbide formation at elevated tempering temperatures.

This figure presents six scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates formed via spinodal decomposition in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels tempered at 800°C. Subfigures (a) and (b) display agglomerated acicular nano-sized κ-carbides in a medium-dense grid within alloy 9Al-10Mn-1C at magnifications of ×10,000 and ×30,000, respectively. Subfigures (c) and (d) reveal densely packed cuboidal nano-sized κ-carbides with the thickest configuration in alloy 12Al-20Mn-1C, representing the optimal spinodal decomposition morphology. Subfigures (e) and (f) show agglomerated acicular-like κ-carbides with the finest and least dense configuration in alloy 12Al-30Mn-1C. The comparison demonstrates that increasing Mn content from 10% to 20% (with 12% Al) transforms the precipitate morphology from acicular agglomerates to dense cuboidal particles, while further increasing Mn to 30% results in finer, less dense acicular precipitates. These microstructural variations illustrate the strong composition-dependence of spinodal decomposition products and their morphological evolution in austenitic lightweight steels.

This figure presents calculated phase volume fraction diagrams (mole fraction vs. temperature) for two Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels: (a) 9Al-10Mn-0.4C and (b) 9Al-10Mn-1C (in wt%), illustrating thermodynamic gradients governing austenite stability. Both subplots track the equilibrium phase evolution of BCC_A2 (ferrite, α), FCC_A1 (austenite, γ), and KAPPA_E21 (κ-carbide) from 0°C to 1300°C. In (a), BCC_A2 dominates at low temperatures (~0.9 mol fraction) while FCC_A1 remains minimal; a sharp phase transformation occurs around 700°C where BCC_A2 drops precipitously and FCC_A1 rises, reflecting ferrite-to-austenite transformation. In contrast, (b) shows FCC_A1 as the dominant phase at low temperatures (~0.8 mol fraction) for the higher carbon content, with BCC_A2 only appearing above 650°C. The diagrams qualitatively explain the experimental observation that 9Al-10Mn-0.4C undergoes austenite decomposition during tempering at 600°C but not at 800°C, whereas 9Al-10Mn-1C exhibits decomposition at both temperatures due to favorable thermodynamic driving forces indicated by the phase fraction gradients. The κ-carbide phase remains relatively stable (~0.05-0.2 mol fraction) across intermediate temperatures in both alloys.

This figure presents paired optical micrographs illustrating the microstructural characteristics of Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels after tempering at 600°C. Subfigure (a) shows the 9Al-20Mn-1C alloy, revealing austenite grains with prominent parallel striations oriented differently in each grain, alongside grain boundary precipitates that caption and text identify as likely γ+α phases exhibiting continuity with δ-ferrite grains. Subfigure (b) depicts the 9Al-30Mn-1C alloy, displaying similar grain striations but featuring exclusively κ-carbide precipitates along the grain boundaries. Both images include a 20 μm scale bar. The citation sentences indicate that despite the presence of these precipitates, both microstructures retained hardness values identical to their hot-rolled counterparts rather than showing strengthening effects expected from high κ-carbide content. This suggests the striated morphology and precipitation characteristics observed in these high-manganese variants may be detrimental to strength and ductility, unlike fine lamellar structures in other compositions that produced significant hardening.

This scanning electron micrograph displays the microstructure of 9Al-20Mn-1C Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel after tempering at 600°C for 30 minutes, captured at 20 kV with 5,000× magnification (scale bar 5 μm). The image reveals secondary phases—identified in the caption as κ and/or γ-carbides—precipitated along grain boundaries, exhibiting continuity with the adjacent austenite grain. Unlike lower-manganese alloys in the same study that undergo discontinuous precipitation or cellular transformation at this temperature, this high-manganese composition shows no significant bulk austenite decomposition, with only grain boundary precipitation visible. The micrograph supports the finding that 20 wt% Mn stabilizes the austenite phase at 600°C, suppressing lamellar microstructure formation while permitting carbide precipitation at boundaries, correlating with a moderate hardness increase to 359 HV compared to the hot-rolled condition (233 HV).

The figure presents four thermodynamic phase volume fraction diagrams (mole fraction vs. temperature) calculated for Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels with varying Al and Mn content: (a) 9Al-20Mn-1C, (b) 9Al-30Mn-1C, (c) 12Al-20Mn-1C, and (d) 12Al-30Mn-1C. Each subplot tracks the equilibrium phase amounts of FCC_A1 (austenite, γ), BCC_A2 (ferrite, α/δ), KAPPA_E21 (κ-carbide), BCC_2SL (ordered bcc), and LIQUID across 0–1300°C. The diagrams reveal distinct stability windows: for 9Al alloys, the FCC_A1 phase field shifts to lower temperatures with decreasing Mn, while for 12Al alloys, the f.c.c. field moves toward higher temperatures and away from typical tempering temperatures (600–800°C), indicating reduced austenite stability at 800°C compared to 1150°C. The KAPPA_E21 phase persists as a stable phase around 20 mol% at lower temperatures in all compositions but dissolves above 700–800°C. Subplots (c) and (d) additionally show BCC_2SL stability between approximately 500–800°C, which is absent in the 9Al counterparts. These thermodynamic gradients explain the experimental observation that 12Al alloys exhibit higher propensity for austenite decomposition (cellular transformation, spinodal decomposition) during tempering, whereas 9Al-20/30Mn-1C compositions retain higher austenite fractions at 800°C than at 1150°C, indicating greater austenite stability in the lower-Al alloys at typical processing temperatures.

The figure presents three optical micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels after tempering at 800°C for 30 minutes, specifically illustrating the competition between cellular transformation and spinodal decomposition across different alloy compositions. Subfigure (a) depicts the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy, where caption states that cellular transformation predominates over spinodal decomposition due to lower austenite stability associated with the lower Mn content, featuring elongated lamellar colonies. Subfigure (b) shows the 12Al-20Mn-1C alloy, also exhibiting predominantly cellular transformed fractions; citation sentences note that some grains feature mixed structures with ferrite branches extending into spinodally decomposed austenitic regions. Subfigure (c) displays the 12Al-30Mn-1C alloy, where caption reports that only spinodal decomposition has involved the entire austenitic volume, resulting in a uniform fine-scale microstructure with nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates distributed throughout the γ matrix. The systematic transition from cellular transformation to spinodal decomposition with increasing Mn content (from 10 wt% to 30 wt%) demonstrates that higher Mn stabilizes austenite, thereby altering the decomposition mechanism and phase transformation kinetics at 800°C. All micrographs share a scale bar of 400 μm.

The figure presents two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of 12Al-20Mn-1C lightweight steel tempered at 800°C, illustrating a complex mixed microstructure. The left subfigure (×2,500 magnification, 10 μm scale bar) provides a lower-magnification overview showing dark ferrite branches (δ-ferrite) extending vertically from an upper boundary region into the underlying lighter matrix, identified as spinodally decomposed austenite (γ) grains. The right subfigure (×10,000 magnification, 1 μm scale bar) reveals higher-resolution details of the interaction zone, displaying fine-scale modulated structures characteristic of spinodal decomposition within the austenite matrix alongside the intruding lamellar features. Caption states that these ferrite branches are linked to κ-carbide lamellar grains and extend inside spinodal decomposed austenite grains, representing a mixed structure where cellular transformation coexists with spinodal decomposition. This phenomenon demonstrates the competition between transformation mechanisms in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels at elevated temperatures, with the text noting that this specific interaction requires further study for proper understanding.
